speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was bar mitzvahed in April of 1938, which obviously was before the Kristallnacht affair. By that time, the Jewish community had-- which I mentioned earlier-- was 16 families-- had depleted to a large degree. I believe 8 of the families, of the 16, had left Gemunden-- either had immigrated to the United States, South Africa, Argentina, and/or moved into larger cities away from Gemunden, because they didn't want to be exposed to the horrors which were going on in-- in Gemunden itself. And out of the other eight families, four of them were single, older women with their daughters, living in their houses in Gemunden. So consequently, there was not a minyan available anymore. But there were several smaller cities-- smaller villages, within, I would say, about five or six kilometers around Gemunden, which still had Jewish people living there. Most of them were farmers, or they were cattle dealers. They bought and sold cows and horses to the farmers. And they would come on Shabbat for minyan to Gemunden, because we didn't have a minyan in Gemunden anymore, obviously. And for my bar mitzvah, we had a full house. I believe there must have been possibly 20 men, 25 maybe-- including my father's family, who did not live in Gemunden-- had come from-- from the southern part of Germany to visit and celebrate the bar mitzvah with us together. It was a normal affair, as normal as it could have been in 1938, prior to the Kristallnacht. It's still in my memory. I have not talked about it much, but it was a wonderful experience for me. Number one, I studied very hard. Not only did I say one parsha, but I recited the whole sidra of the Torah portion, which was read at my bar mitzvah, which was Kedoshim. And we have Torah as well. So it was a really momentous experience for me, having learned and studied, and then being able to decide the-- the whole Torah portion of my bar mitzvah part. It was a good experience-- wonderful experience. But our religious life, our life at home, did not change at all. Our faith in God, our belief in better days to come, the optimism my father always had, prevailed in our house, although we knew deep down that it was really, really getting from bad to worse. The other Jewish families, the other four Jewish families who lived in Gemunden, their situation got so bad that eventually, they all moved in to our house with us. So by that time, by the end of 1939, the middle of 1939, I believe there were approximately 12 or 13 people living in our house. We all shared the same problems. We had the same experiences, more or less. And I believe it was-- was a tremendous support. We were a tremendous support for each other, because there was no way out. There was just no way out.

speaker: interviewer
text: By that time, my father had abandoned the idea that Hitler wouldn't last very long, which he always was proclaiming through 1934, and '35, and '36. By that time, he had been exposed to the terrible, terrible physical tyranny in November the 9th night that he-- he just said, well, we just have to wait and see what's happening, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had very little contact with Jewish agencies in Frankfurt. Our life was miserable. We couldn't go out on the street, other than go to work. We were stoned. Food was very scarce. After all, we had lived in the country, had a farm, and food was always plentiful. Now we had-- we didn't have any more potatoes, which was the mainstay of our diet, so to speak. Meat was impossible to get. Number one, it was no kosher meat was available. And we wouldn't eat anything else. Till about 1938, we had a shochet come to our house occasionally, and would kill a chicken for us. But that was all done and over with after Kristallnacht. And things became very desperate. Anyhow, my father and I went-- kept on going to work, because that was the only way we could sustain ourselves with food. We wouldn't have had any rations. Plus, it-- it became worse and worse. As the conditions became worse, we decided, my father and mother decided, that I had to leave Gemunden, that I couldn't stay there any longer. I would come home with a bloody head and beaten up-- literally, every day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I made contacts in Frankfurt with some friends of ours. And they shipped me to Frankfurt. I was 14 years by that time. And I had to earn a living in Frankfurt. I worked. I-- I've gotten a job. And I worked for a landscaping company, living in a basement room, with a bed. But nobody knew who I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: The friends were Jewish in Frankfurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were-- they were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those friends were Jewish. Nobody knew who I was till the end of '39 or '40-- I'm not sure what date. Where it was-- became obligatory-- that all of us, all Jews, wear yellow stars on their clothing. And again, things went from bad to worse. Although the people I worked for knew I was Jewish, I had to wear a star going to work, which made things more difficult for me. And I guess it made it difficult for them, too, because they were employing a Jew. Nevertheless, I was able to keep my job, earn whatever marks I was earning, to pay for room and board I had in-- in Frankfurt. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did you encounter much anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Frankfurt is a large city. And there was anti-Semitism, because the same things that happened in Gemunden happened in Frankfurt, too. But in Gemunden, there were only-- in 19-- after 1938, probably 10 Jewish people living. The Jewish populace had decreased, and the Nazi populace had increased. In Frankfurt, you sort of got lost in the crowd. And whenever possible, I would go out not wearing my star, because I really felt I-- I couldn't take it to be harassed constantly. I was a little kid; 14 years old. I was small for my age. And as time went on, you know, you learned how to deal with those situations, particularly as a young boy. You're innovative. And you try to work all angles you possibly can. Anyway, in 1940-- middle of 1940, that also ended. I was discharged, fired from the job I had. The people I had lived with immigrated. I believe they went to Argentina. And my family decided, my mom and dad, decided, I should come back home, which I did. I went back to Gemunden again. From the middle of '40 to the end of 1941, things were constantly getting worse. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The food situation was terrible. Although people still helped us. And that was the only way we could survive. We had very little contact with the outside world. Mail was intercepted. And we didn't have a radio. There was no telephone. Things were really pretty rotten, particularly for a 14-year-old boy who wants to conquer the world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I knew what the situation was. I knew it was bad. And aside from praying, saying I'll pray every day, there was very little we thought we could do to remedy the situation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was still going to work. And as-- when I came back to Gemunden in-- in-- in '40, I had to go back to work with him again, and again, carrying bricks. And this was an ongoing thing-- just to be able to get rations, get food and not to be bothered. They had to get away from Gemunden. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Things sort of-- you know, there-- there-- there's a time in life when you learn to live with your misery. And I guess that's where we had arrived-- all of us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were helpless-- nothing we could do to remedy the situation. There was no help from the outside. We knew that other Jewish communities were suffering as badly as we did. Although we heard that in the southern part of Germany, things weren't as bad as they were by us. But when you think you are-- you're-- you're badly off-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --and are-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --satisfied with your lot-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --you don't realize how well you are off when things got worse, which was in December of 1941. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Believe it was on a Sunday night when the German police, with two Gestapo men-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --came to our house on Sunday afternoon and told us that tomorrow morning, Monday morning, you will be resettled. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Resettled, meaning what? Well, you're not doing us any good here. And we need you for the war effort. Obviously, the war had started already. I don't think I have to cover that. That's common knowledge. And we need to resettle you to help us in our war effort. Where are we going? Well, they were noncommittal.

speaker: interviewee
text: All they told us, by tomorrow morning, at six o'clock, have a suitcase ready with whatever things you think you need. And we'll take you away from here. We'll resettle you. There was my father, my mother, my sister-- who had been in Gemunden all this time while I was in Frankfurt-- my two aunts, plus the other six or eight people who lived in our house. Everybody have a suitcase ready. We'll take you, pick you up tomorrow morning at six o'clock. We'll resettle you. In any event, my mother questioned the policeman who she knew. She had gone to school with him. And he said, well, Bella, don't worry about it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They're probably gonna resettle you somewhere where you could be-- going to be working on the land doing farming. Because a lot of men are in a war, and they need people to work the land.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we accepted that-- nothing we could do, no place we could go, no-- no place to hide, no place to run to. Sure enough, we packed our suitcases. And what do you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's a house we lived in. My grandparents lived in this house. My great-grandparents lived in that house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There were things in that house for over 150 years, which belonged to the family. And you're allowed to take a little suitcase. What do you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, all you can take is clothes and some food. Whatever little food we had, we took along, because we didn't know when and where our next meal was gonna to come from. Six o'clock next morning came-- same SS man, same policeman, with a truck. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --they made us go out of the house-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --with our little suitcase we carried, because they told us not more than you can carry. And we didn't have many suitcases. There was no reason to have a suitcase. So some of the things we took were packed in boxes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they put us on a truck with whatever little packages and suitcases we had; and the tears and stress-- horrendous emotional strain-- not knowing where we-- where we're going. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a lot of commotion and crying going on. And you know, when you're 14 years old, you understand it, and you don't understand it. I knew what was happening at the moment, but the significance of it did not enter my mind. Sure, we were taken away to be resettled, to work on a farm. And sure, we knew how to do that. We've been farmers all our lives. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well-- [SIGHS] --they put us on this truck. And about four hours later, we arrived in Kassel, which was the large-- was a large city with a large Jewish community. And they deposited us, or unloaded us, in a huge hall, where we found approximately 600 or 700 other Jewish people from other communities, as well as from Kassel, who had been told they were gonna be resettled-- no beds, no mattresses, just our little suitcase and us in his large hall, which was a [NON-ENGLISH], which was a gymnastics hall-- parquet floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nobody knew really what was going on-- a lot of panic, a lot of panic. And what's your destiny? You know, you don't know. Some rumors went around that we were going here, or there, or somewhere else. And that was-- it was-- it was terrible. It was-- I mean, it was an exposure for me which just words cannot describe-- old people, 60s and their 70s, children, babies, commotion, crying, no food, no water, sleeping on the floor, more Jews coming. What's going on? Nobody knows.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's-- it's-- if you know what's-- what you're up against, you know, you can deal with it. But the unknown-- the unknown is the mystery in life. And after what had been happening, we really weren't sure what they were up to. We hadn't heard about concentration camps. We had heard about [NON-ENGLISH], which is resettling. But we never heard from those people who had been resettled. So they must have been all right. Otherwise, we would have heard from them. Anyhow, we were in this hall for two days. We did get some food and some water. Sanitary conditions were fairly decent, because the Germans didn't want to have their hall messed up. So they made sure that we went to the toilets and bathrooms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They, again, got us up one morning. And I don't recall it for sure, but I believe this hall was very close to a railroad station. They march us out, and everybody there were told-- everybody hold onto their suitcases. They marched us out to this railway station, or to railroad cars-- I don't know if it was a station or not-- to those railroad cars, which were passenger railroad cars. And they packed us in those cars. It was December. It was very cold. The cars weren't heated. When I say packed, they packed us into those cars. But anyone who had a suitcase was told that the railroad cars were too small for suitcases to accommodate them, that they had a freight car in which they were going to load those suitcases into. And at our destinations, we would get our suitcases back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, whatever belongings, worldly belongings, we had, we had to give up to be put into the railroad car, into the freight car, which supposedly was at the end of the train. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Just the thought of it, of thinking back of it-- and I haven't thought of this particular event in a long, long time. It just-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --just--

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. It--

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Seeing my parents, and seeing my aunts, and seeing my sister, and other people being in that railroad car, packed in sardines, cold-- destination unknown; even I then, as a child of 14 years, I think that was the first time where it really bothered me-- sunk into my mind-- into my head, where are they taking you? Where are you going? What's it going to be? How much worse can it be than what you have gone through? It's probably going to be better. It has to be better.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're going to resettle you. You're gonna go work-- gonna work on a farm. You're gonna have a place to live in. You're gonna have food to eat. And we're gonna be amongst ourselves-- amongst Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they're all gonna be productive. And they're not gonna to be bothering us. What else can there be?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, that's the humane or human thing, which a human being can expect from another. I could see that they needed us, because of the war effort. I could see that they needed to resettle us, because we weren't productive where we were, except for ourselves. They wanted us to be productive for the fatherland. I could see all of that. But I saw a light at the end of the tunnel. I saw that, hey. We're really getting out of misery into a better situations-- situation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if these views were shared with me, with many other people. It was of the thought which went through my mind. There was very little talk-- very little spoken, very little discussion.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess everybody was in themselves, within themselves, thinking and hoping that where we were going was a better place than we were coming from. Because place we were coming from was terrible. How much worse can it get?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train left Kassel, and-- oh, God, I can't-- shaken about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [DRINKS] The train left Kassel-- destination unknown. It was very cold-- no steam, no air, no warmth. And train rode into the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's all cuddle together with our overcoats and whatever we had. Whatever little food we had with us, we shared-- no food on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first-- to my-- best to my-- best of my recollection-- first place we knew, or I knew, we were told, or seemingly, we looked out of the window. It was early in the morning. I don't know if it was the second or first day-- second or the first day it was of our trip. We were in Pilsen. And everybody said we were going east. Nobody knew before if we were going east, west, south, or north. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How far? How much further east are we going? Nobody knew.
